revision.comment: typo: Rōmaji (via [[WP:JWB]])
revision.text:
{{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両界曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両部曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the [[Diamond Realm]] as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the [[Womb Realm]]. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[Dharma]] in [[Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[India]], and were joined for the first time in [[China]], perhaps by [[Kūkai]]'s teacher [[Hui-kuo]].
== See also == *[[Dharmadhatu]]
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104619/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/garbhadatu-0.htm The Diamond and Womb World Mandalas] Dharmapala Thangka Centre *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104631/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/antique.htm Mandala of the Womb World] Dharmapala Thangka Centre
{{Buddhism topics}}
[[Category:Buddhist iconography]] [[Category:Mandalas]]

{{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両界曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両部曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the [[Diamond Realm]] as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the [[Womb Realm]]. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[Dharma]] in [[Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[India]], and were joined for the first time in [[China]], perhaps by [[Kūkai]]'s teacher [[Hui-kuo]].

{{buddhism}}

The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[Dharma]] in [[Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[India]], and were joined for the first time in [[China]], perhaps by [[Kūkai]]'s teacher [[Hui-kuo]].

{{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両界曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ([[Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両部曼荼羅; [[Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; [[Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the [[Diamond Realm]] as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the [[Womb Realm]]. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[Dharma]] in [[Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[India]], and were joined for the first time in [[China]], perhaps by [[Kūkai]]'s teacher [[Hui-kuo]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh_yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[sn:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q468113]] Wikidata name: Mandala of the Two Realms [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ([[:en:Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両界曼荼羅; [[:en:Pinyin|Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; [[:en:Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ([[:en:Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両部曼荼羅; [[:en:Pinyin|Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; [[:en:Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the [[:en:Diamond Realm|Diamond Realm]] as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the [[:en:Womb Realm|Womb Realm]]. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[:en:Dharma|Dharma]] in [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[:en:Vajrayana|Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[:en:Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[:en:India|India]], and were joined for the first time in [[:en:China|China]], perhaps by [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].
== See also == *[[:en:Dharmadhatu|Dharmadhatu]]
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104619/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/garbhadatu-0.htm The Diamond and Womb World Mandalas] Dharmapala Thangka Centre *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104631/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/antique.htm Mandala of the Womb World] Dharmapala Thangka Centre

The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ([[:en:Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両界曼荼羅; [[:en:Pinyin|Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; [[:en:Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ([[:en:Traditional Chinese characters|Traditional Chinese]]: 両部曼荼羅; [[:en:Pinyin|Pinyin]]: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; [[:en:Romanization of Japanese|Rōmaji]]: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the [[:en:Diamond Realm|Diamond Realm]] as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the [[:en:Womb Realm|Womb Realm]]. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the [[:en:Dharma|Dharma]] in [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] Buddhism, and form the root of the [[:en:Vajrayana|Vajrayana]] teachings. Chinese [[:en:Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|Tangmi]] and Japanese [[Shingon]] and [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in [[:en:India|India]], and were joined for the first time in [[:en:China|China]], perhaps by [[:en:Kūkai|Kūkai]]'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].
== See also == *[[:en:Dharmadhatu|Dharmadhatu]]
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104619/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/garbhadatu-0.htm The Diamond and Womb World Mandalas] Dharmapala Thangka Centre *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104631/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/antique.htm Mandala of the Womb World] Dharmapala Thangka Centre
{{Buddhism topics}}

The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese}}: 両界曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji}}: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese}}: 両部曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji}}: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the {{ill|Diamond Realm|en|Diamond Realm|WD=Q518314|lt=Diamond Realm}} as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|WD=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm}}. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma}} in {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana}} Buddhism, and form the root of the {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|WD=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana}} teachings. Chinese {{ill|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|en|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|WD=Q10922707|lt=Tangmi}} and Japanese [[Shingon]] and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India}}, and were joined for the first time in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, perhaps by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[co:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[co:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[sn:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q468113]] Wikidata name: Mandala of the Two Realms [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese}}: 両界曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji}}: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese}}: 両部曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji}}: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the {{ill|Diamond Realm|en|Diamond Realm|WD=Q518314|lt=Diamond Realm}} as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|WD=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm}}. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma}} in {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana}} Buddhism, and form the root of the {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|WD=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana}} teachings. Chinese {{ill|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|en|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|WD=Q10922707|lt=Tangmi}} and Japanese [[Shingon]] and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India}}, and were joined for the first time in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, perhaps by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].
== See also == *{{ill|Dharmadhatu|en|Dharmadhatu|WD=Q324750|lt=Dharmadhatu}}
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104619/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/garbhadatu-0.htm The Diamond and Womb World Mandalas] Dharmapala Thangka Centre *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104631/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/antique.htm Mandala of the Womb World] Dharmapala Thangka Centre
{{Buddhism topics}}

The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India}}, and were joined for the first time in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, perhaps by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai}}'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].

{{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese|1=Traditional Chinese characters}}: 両界曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese|1=Traditional Chinese characters}}: 両部曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the {{ill|Diamond Realm|en|Diamond Realm|WD=Q518314|lt=Diamond Realm|1=Diamond Realm}} as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|WD=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}}. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}} in {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} Buddhism, and form the root of the {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|WD=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} teachings. Chinese {{ill|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|en|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|WD=Q10922707|lt=Tangmi|1=Chinese Esoteric Buddhism}} and Japanese [[Shingon]] and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}, and were joined for the first time in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, perhaps by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[co:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[co:commons:Category:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:দুই ক্ষেত্র মণ্ডল]][[en:Mandala of the Two Realms]][[fr:Mandala des deux royaumes]][[hu:A két birodalom mandalája]][[ja:両界曼荼羅]][[ta:இரு பிரிவு மண்டலம்]][[zh-yue:兩界曼陀羅]][[zh:兩部曼荼羅]]
{{buddhism}}
The '''Mandala of the Two Realms''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese|1=Traditional Chinese characters}}: 両界曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngjiè màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Ryōkai mandara''), also known as the '''Mandala of the Two Divisions''' ({{ill|Traditional Chinese characters|en|Traditional Chinese characters|WD=Q178528|lt=Traditional Chinese|1=Traditional Chinese characters}}: 両部曼荼羅; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|WD=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Liǎngbù màntúluó''; {{ill|Romanization of Japanese|en|Romanization of Japanese|WD=Q192509|lt=Rōmaji|1=Romanization of Japanese}}: ''Ryōbu mandara''), is a set of two [[mandala]]s depicting both the [[:en:Five Wisdom Buddhas|Five Wisdom Buddhas]] of the {{ill|Diamond Realm|en|Diamond Realm|WD=Q518314|lt=Diamond Realm|1=Diamond Realm}} as well as the [[Wisdom King|Five Wisdom Kings]] of the {{ill|Womb Realm|en|Womb Realm|WD=Q855739|lt=Womb Realm|1=Womb Realm}}. The number of deities arranged around the cores varies, but may range as high as 414.
The Diamond Realm represents the unchanging cosmic principle of the Buddha, while the Womb Realm depicts the active, physical manifestation of Buddha in the natural world. The mandalas are thus considered a compact expression of the entirety of the {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}} in {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|WD=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} Buddhism, and form the root of the {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|WD=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} teachings. Chinese {{ill|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|en|Chinese Esoteric Buddhism|WD=Q10922707|lt=Tangmi|1=Chinese Esoteric Buddhism}} and Japanese [[Shingon]] and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} temples, in particular, often prominently display the Mandalas of the Two Realms mounted at right angles to the image platform on the central altar.
The two mandalas are believed to have evolved separately in {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}, and were joined for the first time in {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, perhaps by {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|WD=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}'s teacher [[:en:Hui-kuo|Hui-kuo]].
== See also == *{{ill|Dharmadhatu|en|Dharmadhatu|WD=Q324750|lt=Dharmadhatu|1=Dharmadhatu}}
== External links == *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104619/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/garbhadatu-0.htm The Diamond and Womb World Mandalas] Dharmapala Thangka Centre *[https://web.archive.org/web/20110728104631/http://www.thangka.de/Gallery-3/Cosmos/9-6/antique.htm Mandala of the Womb World] Dharmapala Thangka Centre
{{Buddhism topics}}
